input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The **Physical Twin system** being studied is a **complex engineered system** (e.g., manufacturing or industrial system) characterized by the following key attributes: **(1) Multi-layered abstraction**: The system is modeled across distinct abstraction levels (requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layer) to manage complexity, as per **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies like **RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics)**, **OOSEM**, **SPES**, or **SMArDT**. **(2) Component-based architecture**: The system is decomposed into **components and connectors**, described using **Architecture Description Languages (ADLs)** such as **ACME**, **AADL**, **MontiArc**, or **SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs)**. These ADLs explicitly define **interfaces, interconnections, and composition** of components. **(3) Behavioral modeling**: The system’s behavior is captured using **state machines (SysML v1.7)** or **MontiArc Automaton**, often supplemented by **simulation tools** (e.g., **MathWorks Simulink**) or **general-purpose programming languages (C++, Java)** for detailed dynamic analysis. **(4) Heterogeneous stakeholder integration**: The system involves **diverse stakeholders and perspectives**, requiring unification of models across **different notations and tools**. **(5) Operational domain**: Primarily aligned with **manufacturing systems**, where the **Digital Shadow Reference Model** (as defined by Kritzinger et al.) emphasizes **contextual data traces** (e.g., sensor data, operational logs) aggregated for **specific purposes** (e.g., monitoring, diagnostics, or process optimization). The system’s suitability for digital twinning stems from its **data-driven, component-oriented, and behaviorally explicit** nature, enabling **bidirectional interaction** (unlike passive digital shadows).
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The **Digital Twin (DT)** provides the following **specific services** to users and the physical system, categorized by functionality and target audience: **(1) Data Acquisition & Abstraction**: Actively collects, aggregates, and abstracts **contextual data traces** (e.g., sensor readings, operational states) from the physical system, unlike passive **Digital Shadows**, which only enable one-way data flow. **(2) Monitoring & Diagnostics**: Enables **real-time or near-real-time monitoring** of system components and their interconnections, leveraging **ADL-defined interfaces** and **behavioral models** (e.g., state machines) to detect anomalies or deviations. **(3) Simulation & Prediction**: Integrates with **simulation tools** (e.g., **MathWorks Simulink**, other simulation engines) to **predict system behavior**, validate designs, or optimize operational parameters under varying conditions. **(4) Control & Optimization**: Provides **active components** (e.g., automated logic modules) to **interpret data** and **adjust physical system parameters** (e.g., via control loops or optimization algorithms), unlike digital shadows that lack autonomous operation. **(5) Visualization & Decision Support**: Offers **multi-perspective visualization** of system models (e.g., functional/logical architectures, simulation results) to support **stakeholder-specific decision-making**, aligning with **MBSE methodologies** like **SysML** or **MontiArc**. **(6) Cross-Domain Integration**: Facilitates **heterogeneous tool integration** (e.g., combining **ADLs for architecture**, **simulation tools for dynamics**, and **code for custom logic**) to unify disparate data sources and models. **Target users** include **system engineers** (for design/validation), **operators** (for real-time monitoring), and **managers** (for strategic optimization).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The implementation of the **Digital Twin** relies on a **heterogeneous toolchain** and **enabling technologies**, categorized by their role in the system lifecycle: **(1) Modeling & Architecture Tools**:
    - **SysML (Systems Modeling Language)**: Used for **multi-layered abstraction** (requirements, functional/logical architectures) and **behavioral modeling** (state machines in v1.7).
    - **Architecture Description Languages (ADLs)**: **ACME**, **AADL**, **MontiArc** (with **MontiArc Automaton** for behavioral modeling), and **SysML IBDs** to define **components, connectors, and interfaces**.
    - **Methodologies**: **RFLP**, **OOSEM**, **SPES**, or **SMArDT** for structuring **complex system development**.
  **(2) Simulation & Analysis Tools**:
    - **MathWorks Simulink**: For **detailed dynamic simulation** and **control system modeling**.
    - **Other Simulation Engines**: Generic or domain-specific tools (e.g., **ANSYS**, **COMSOL**) for **physics-based validation**.
  **(3) Programming & Integration**:
    - **General-Purpose Languages**: **C++** or **Java** for implementing **custom logic**, **control algorithms**, or **data processing pipelines**.
    - **Model Integration Frameworks**: Tools to **unify heterogeneous models** (e.g., **FMI**, **DDS**, or **OPC UA** for interoperability).
  **(4) Data Management**:
    - **Databases**: Likely **time-series databases** (e.g., **InfluxDB**, **TimescaleDB**) or **graph databases** (e.g., **Neo4j**) for storing **contextual data traces** and **aggregated metadata**.
    - **Data Abstraction Layers**: Custom or **ETL (Extract, Transform, Load)** tools to process raw sensor data into **actionable insights**.
  **(5) Digital Twin-Specific Enablers**:
    - **Active Components**: Software modules (e.g., **automated agents**, **rule engines**) to **interpret data** and **trigger actions** (e.g., alerts, adjustments) in the physical system.
    - **Digital Shadow Reference Model**: Leverages **UML-based class diagrams** (as per Kritzinger et al.) to define **data aggregation** and **abstraction** for manufacturing contexts.
  **(6) Stakeholder Collaboration**:
    - **Model Exchange Formats**: **XMI**, **FMI**, or **JSON/XML schemas** to ensure **interoperability** across tools (e.g., SysML to Simulink).
    - **Collaborative Platforms**: **Model-based collaboration tools** (e.g., **Jinja**, ** Cameo Systems Modeler**) for **multi-stakeholder alignment**.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The provided document does not explicitly detail multiplicities or hierarchical structures of Digital Twin (DT) instances. However, it implies a **centralized vs. decentralized architecture** through the use of **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies (e.g., RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, SMArDT) and **architecture description languages (ADLs)** like ACME, AADL, or MontiArc, which may support **component-based decomposition** and **composition hierarchies**. The document also references **SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs)** for logical architecture modeling, which inherently supports **multiplicity definitions** (e.g., 1-to-many relationships between components). Coordination mechanisms are not explicitly described, but the integration of **heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives** suggests a need for **distributed responsibility scopes** across DT instances. No specific synchronization frequencies, latency requirements, or temporal granularity are provided.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The **Digital Twin (DT) constellation** is implicitly defined through the **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** framework, which employs **multi-layered abstraction** (requirements, functional, logical, and product/technical layers) and **architecture description languages (ADLs)** like ACME, AADL, or MontiArc to orchestrate system-wide coordination. The document highlights the use of **component & connector perspectives** (e.g., interfaces, interconnections, and composition) to manage **resource management** and **system integration**. Behavioral descriptions are supported via **state machines (SysML v1.7)** or **MontiArc Automaton**, enabling **dynamic coordination** between components. The integration of **simulation tools (e.g., MathWorks Simulink)** and **general-purpose languages (C++, Java)** further suggests a **hybrid orchestration** approach, combining **model-driven and code-driven execution**. However, no explicit architecture patterns (e.g., microservices, service-oriented) or system-wide coordination protocols (e.g., event-driven, publish-subscribe) are detailed.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The document emphasizes **horizontal integration** through the **interoperability challenges** inherent in MBSE, where **heterogeneous modeling notations** (e.g., SysML, ADLs) and **tools** (Simulink, C++, Java) must coexist. **Data exchange protocols** are not explicitly named, but the reliance on **ADLs (e.g., AADL, MontiArc)** and **SysML IBDs** implies adherence to **standardized interface definitions** (e.g., ports, connectors) for **inter-system communication**. The document also references **digital shadow integration** (via one-way data flow), suggesting dependencies on **external physical systems** and **enterprise data aggregation** mechanisms. No specific interoperability standards (e.g., OPC UA, MQTT) or integration patterns (e.g., API gateways, ESBs) are mentioned, but the **passive data collection** model of digital shadows implies a need for **external active components** (e.g., middleware) to process and distribute data across DTs or enterprise systems.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin (DT) system integrates **computational models and data components** across multiple abstraction layers, leveraging **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies. Key model types include **geometric models** (e.g., CAD representations), **behavioral models** (e.g., state machines in SysML v1.7 or MontiArc Automaton), and **analytical models** (e.g., simulations in MathWorks Simulink or other simulation tools like those referenced in [29], [30]). Data sources are heterogeneous, encompassing **real-time sensor feeds**, **historical operational data**, and **stakeholder-specific inputs**. Data management approaches involve **architecture description languages (ADLs)** such as **ACME, AADL, MontiArc, or SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs)** to define **component interfaces, interconnections, and composition**. Model relationships are established through **hierarchical abstraction layers** (e.g., RFLP: Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics [18], [19]) and **methodologies like OOSEM, SPES, or SMArDT [20]–[22]**. The DT constellation integrates these models via **cross-tool interoperability**, often requiring translation between **SysML, ADLs, simulation tools (e.g., Simulink), and general-purpose programming languages (e.g., C++, Java)** to ensure consistency across stakeholders and perspectives [31].
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The Digital Twin system incorporates **fidelity and validity considerations** through structured **validation and verification (V&V) methods** embedded in MBSE workflows. **Model fidelity** is ensured by aligning computational models (e.g., behavioral/analytical models in SysML or Simulink) with **physical system requirements**, while **uncertainty sources** (e.g., sensor noise, model approximations) are quantified via **statistical analysis or simulation-based error propagation** (e.g., using tools like Simulink or domain-specific simulators [29], [30]). **Error detection and correction mechanisms** may involve **automated anomaly detection** in data streams (e.g., from digital shadows) or **model calibration loops** where physical feedback refines digital representations. **Quality assurance processes** include **traceability matrices** (linking requirements to models) and **cross-validation** between heterogeneous tools (e.g., SysML for architecture, Simulink for dynamics). The system’s **digital shadow reference model** (Fig. 1) implicitly supports validity by aggregating **contextual data traces** for abstraction, though explicit V&V methodologies are not detailed in the provided context.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The **physical-to-virtual interaction** in a Digital Twin (DT) system is characterized by an **automated one-way data flow** from the physical object to its digital representation, as defined in the Digital Shadow Reference Model [32]. This flow involves the **collection of contextual data traces** (e.g., sensor readings, operational states, environmental parameters) from the physical system, which are then **aggregated, abstracted, and stored** for specific analytical or monitoring purposes. The data transmission is **passive**, meaning no bidirectional control or feedback is inherent to the digital shadow itself. Preprocessing steps may include **data validation, normalization, and filtering** to ensure **data quality assurance** before integration into the digital model. The interaction relies on **heterogeneous data sources** (e.g., IoT sensors, PLCs, or SCADA systems) and may leverage **standardized communication protocols** (e.g., OPC UA, MQTT, or HTTP/REST) for transmission. The **frequency of data updates** depends on the application domain (e.g., real-time for critical systems, periodic for non-critical ones), with **event triggers** (e.g., threshold breaches, scheduled intervals) dictating data acquisition. Integration mechanisms involve **modeling tools** like SysML (for structural/behavioral modeling) or simulation environments (e.g., MathWorks Simulink) to incorporate the data into the DT’s functional and logical architecture layers [17], [28].
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The **technical network infrastructure** for a Digital Twin (DT) system is not explicitly detailed in the provided documents. However, the broader context of **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** and **Digital Shadow Reference Model** implies reliance on **heterogeneous communication protocols** (e.g., OPC UA for industrial automation, MQTT for IoT, or HTTP/REST for cloud services) to facilitate data exchange between physical and digital layers. The **network topology** may adopt a **hybrid architecture** (e.g., edge computing for low-latency local processing and cloud for centralized analytics), with **bandwidth and latency requirements** varying by use case (e.g., real-time control demanding sub-100ms latency). **Reliability and redundancy mechanisms** (e.g., failover protocols, data replication) are critical to ensure uninterrupted operation, while **security protocols** (e.g., TLS/SSL, role-based access control) must safeguard data integrity and confidentiality during transmission. The infrastructure must support **interoperability** across tools (e.g., SysML, Simulink, or general-purpose languages like C++/Java) used in DT development [17], [28], [31].
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The **hosting and deployment infrastructure** for a Digital Twin (DT) system is not explicitly specified in the provided documents. However, the **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** framework suggests a **multi-layered deployment model**, potentially leveraging **cloud, edge, or on-premise platforms** depending on computational and storage demands. **Scalability mechanisms** (e.g., containerization via Docker, orchestration via Kubernetes) may be employed to handle dynamic workloads, while **availability/reliability assurance** could involve redundancy, load balancing, and high-availability clustering. The deployment must integrate with **heterogeneous tools** (e.g., SysML for architecture modeling, Simulink for simulation, or C++/Java for custom logic), requiring **interoperability standards** (e.g., OMG’s SysML, FIWARE for IoT) to unify disparate components. The choice of platform (e.g., AWS IoT Greengrass for edge, Azure Digital Twins for cloud) would depend on the DT’s **real-time processing needs, data volume, and stakeholder accessibility** [17], [28], [31].
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering and development process for a Digital Twin (DT) leverages **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies, utilizing systems engineering modeling languages such as **SysML** for structuring system descriptions across multiple abstraction layers (e.g., requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers). Methodologies like **RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics)**, **OOSEM**, **SPES**, or **SMArDT** are employed to manage complexity. For functional and logical architecture, **Architecture Description Languages (ADLs)** such as **ACME**, **AADL**, **MontiArc** (including MontiArc Automaton for behavioral modeling), or **SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs)** are used to define components, interfaces, interconnections, and composition. Behavioral details are further refined using tools like **MathWorks Simulink**, other simulation tools (e.g., [29], [30]), or general-purpose programming languages (e.g., **C++**, **Java**). The process integrates heterogeneous stakeholder perspectives and tools, requiring seamless interoperability between modeling notations and platforms. **Quality assurance** is implicitly tied to the rigor of MBSE methodologies, while **testing and validation** rely on simulation-driven approaches (e.g., Simulink) and formal verification techniques. **Version control** and **evolution management** are not explicitly detailed but are inferred to depend on the underlying MBSE toolchain’s capabilities (e.g., SysML’s model management features or versioning in ADLs).
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The Digital Twin system adheres to **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** standards, particularly leveraging **SysML** (Systems Modeling Language) for system description and **Architecture Description Languages (ADLs)** such as **ACME**, **AADL**, **MontiArc**, and **SysML IBDs** for component-based modeling. Interoperability is facilitated through integration with simulation tools like **MathWorks Simulink** and general-purpose programming languages (**C++**, **Java**), ensuring compliance with broader software engineering standards. While the document does not specify exact **certification frameworks** or **industry standards** (e.g., ISO, IEC, or domain-specific norms like **IEC 62443** for cybersecurity or **ISO 22400** for manufacturing), the use of **MBSE** and **ADLs** implies alignment with **systems engineering best practices** (e.g., **INCOSE** standards). **Interoperability standards** are indirectly supported by the modular and tool-agnostic nature of MBSE, enabling data exchange across heterogeneous systems. The **Digital Shadow Reference Model** (Fig. 1) further suggests adherence to **UML-based modeling standards** for class diagrams and data aggregation.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The Digital Twin system for the MABI MAX 100 serial robot incorporates **safety-critical system considerations** through the integration of a **safety cell** that restricts the maximum working range of the first vertical axis to **[-90°, 90°]**, ensuring operational boundaries are enforced. **Fail-safe mechanisms** are implicitly addressed via the **dual encoder system** (motor encoder and secondary encoder behind the gearbox), which mitigates backlash and positional uncertainty by cross-referencing encoder data to infer elasticity and friction in the drive train. The **Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl CNC controller** provides **cybersecurity and safety protocols** by enabling controlled access to axis inputs (position, speed, acceleration) and outputs (target/actual position, derivatives, motor current), though explicit details on authentication, threat mitigation, or compliance with regulations (e.g., ISO 26262, IEC 62443) are not specified. **Risk assessment** is indirectly supported by the system’s ability to derive torque (τ_M, τ_G, τ_out) from motor current (I_M) using known motor constants, enabling predictive maintenance and fault detection. No explicit mention of **data anonymization**, **consent management**, or **data sharing agreements** is provided.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

1) _Problem identification:_ To get all relevant requirements,
one has to describe the existing problem, the scope of the
considerations, the scope of the solution, and the purpose
why a DS is created.
2) _Asset and model analysis:_ One has to choose the parts of
the asset that are relevant for the defined DS purpose and
analyze from which data sources the data needed can be
covered. This leads to a description of the asset and the
data structure.

3) _Use or creation of calculations or models:_ As existing
data might not be sufficient to fulfil the purpose, we



can use existing or create new calculations, simulation
models, or data aggregation methods.
4) _Data identification:_ We have to identify relevant data
traces, data points, and their metadata.


III. U SE C ASE


The focus of this paper is the serial robot MABI MAX
100 with six axes from the Swiss company MABI Robotic
AG (Fig. 2 top). Due to its flexible rotary joints, high path
repeatability (0 _._ 06 mm) and positional accuracy (0 _._ 1 mm), as
well as its functional reach of 2 m, it is suitable for complex
manufacturing tasks such as machining [44]. The overarching
research task of this industrial robot is machining. The first
vertical robot axis at the base is used for creating a digital
shadow.

Fig. 2 bottom sketches the functional structure. The first
axis is driven by a permanent magnet synchronous motor with
the current _I_ _M_ as input and torque _τ_ _M_ as output. To achieve
the necessary low gear ratio, a gear transmission is first used
(with torque _τ_ _G_ as output), followed by a cycloidal gear (with
torque _τ_ _out_ as output). The safety cell of the robot restricts the
maximum working range to [ _−_ 90 _[◦]_ _,_ 90 _[◦]_ ], and the maximum
achievable speed is _|q_ ˙ max _|_ = 75 _[◦]_ _/_ s. There are two position
encoders per drive. The motor encoder is mounted directly
on the motor, the secondary encoder behind the gearbox.
This sensor arrangement can reduce backlash and positional
uncertainty, as the difference between these encoders allows
conclusions to be drawn about the elasticity and friction of the
entire drive train.

The robot is controlled by the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D
sl. This Computer Numerical Control (CNC) controller is
particularly suitable for subtractive manufacturing processes
such as milling. The axes can be controlled separately. Position, speed, or acceleration can serve as inputs. Possible
outputs are the target and actual values of the position and its
derivatives, as well as the motor current. The motor current
can be converted into the applied torque using known motor

constants.
